questions.question: What is the biggest risk of jumping straight into architecture before clarification?
options: You may optimize for the wrong requirement and spend the interview defending a mismatched design.
You will always choose the wrong database engine on the first attempt.
The interviewer will stop the interview because diagrams are not allowed.
You will be forced to discuss coding details instead of architecture.
questions.explanation: Without clarification, the architecture can drift away from the real prompt and become expensive to recover.

questions.question: Why is rough sizing early in the answer valuable?
options: It replaces the need for a high-level architecture diagram.
It helps drive topology, bottleneck, and cost decisions before details pile up.
It guarantees the final latency numbers are production accurate.
It is only useful for storage-heavy interview prompts.
questions.explanation: Sizing gives the design numerical anchors so component choices are tied to scale and constraints.

questions.question: In the default 45-minute loop, what should happen before the deep dive?
options: A detailed schema migration plan and postmortem draft.
A high-level design with buy-in plus rough sizing and clarified scope.
Final trade-off summary and executive wrap-up.
Implementation pseudocode for the write path.
questions.explanation: The deep dive should come after scope, sizing, and high-level architecture are aligned.

questions.question: Which follow-up best shows senior-level control of the interview?
options: Listing additional infrastructure components as fast as possible.
Choosing one critical subsystem to deep dive and saying why it is the system's main risk.
Deferring all trade-offs until the very end so the architecture stays simple.
Avoiding assumptions until the interviewer supplies exact numbers.
questions.explanation: A deliberate deep dive shows prioritization, not just breadth.

questions.question: What is the value of explicitly reserving wrap-up time?
options: It leaves space for failure modes, observability, and trade-offs that weaker answers often skip.
It prevents the interviewer from asking any questions about cost.
It guarantees there will be no redesign follow-ups.
It allows the candidate to avoid discussing system limitations.
questions.explanation: Strong answers do not end at the diagram; they close with operational thinking and known limits.

questions.question: If the interviewer changes the scale from 100K QPS to 10M QPS halfway through, what should you do first?
options: Ignore the new constraint and finish the original answer quickly.
Restart the interview from scratch with a different prompt.
Call out which assumptions changed and identify the first architectural choices that would no longer hold.
Immediately replace every storage component with a globally replicated database.
questions.explanation: A good redesign starts by naming which assumptions broke and what those changes affect.

questions.question: Which observability signal is most useful for improving interview pacing over time?
options: Minutes spent in each stage and whether wrap-up was reached consistently.
The exact number of boxes drawn per architecture diagram.
The total number of services mentioned in the answer.
The number of times the interviewer interrupted the candidate.
questions.explanation: Tracking stage timing helps diagnose whether the answer structure is balanced.

questions.question: What trade-off is introduced by using a fixed interview loop?
options: It increases storage cost but reduces bandwidth cost.
It adds structure and consistency, but can feel rigid if applied without adapting to the prompt.
It removes the need for any deep dive selection.
It makes clarification unnecessary when time is short.
questions.explanation: The structure is useful, but strong candidates still adapt it rather than using it mechanically.
